intent: fork an upstream GitHub repo, clone my fork locally, and run a security review before I adopt or build on it
finding: Three sessions in this window opened with essentially the same request shape: 'fork + clone locally + security review' a named upstream repo (mcp-memory-service, thedotmack/claude-mem, voquill/voquill). Each time Claude had to re-derive the sequence (gh auth/fork, git clone fork, invoke security review, summarize findings, cross-check against open issues) from scratch. The outcome is the same each time — a vetted local fork ready to adopt — and it is exactly the kind of task a single skill or script could orchestrate once-and-for-all. The `security-review` skill covers the review step; what's missing is the wrapper that takes a URL and handles the fork/clone/review/report chain.
existing_mitigation: `~/.claude/skills/security-review/` handles the review step; no skill/script wires together the `gh fork → git clone → run security-review → summary` chain.

intent: make autodoro correctly detect when I am actively dictating, regardless of which speech-to-text app I am using
finding: After migrating from whisper-writer to voquill for dictation, autodoro's 'is user speaking' detection stopped suppressing the pomodoro ring because its allowlist was hard-coded to whisper-writer. The user drove a long interactive loop — triggering voquill, waiting, reading back pactl source-output dumps, asking Claude multiple times 'did it trigger?' / 'what is the status now?' — with visible frustration ('ANSWER MY DIRECT WUESTION'). The durable fix is either a process-agnostic speaker-active detector or an allowlist config that the user (or the installer of each new STT app) can extend without code changes. The recurrence surface is clear: every time the user swaps STT tools, this breaks again.
existing_mitigation: autodoro has a per-app exemption for whisper-writer but no mechanism to extend the list without code changes; no config file surface exposed.

intent: wait for a dev server (vite/tauri/etc.) to finish building and confirm it is actually serving before I act on it
finding: During the voquill session the user asked Claude three to four times in a row to 'check /tmp/voquill-dev.log tail … report dev server status' / 'check for vite ready line' before moving on. Each check was the same tail+grep pattern against the same log. This is the recurring-across-sessions shape of 'is my dev server actually up yet' — common to every Vite/Next/Tauri project. A small helper (wait-for-log-pattern with timeout, or a tauri/vite 'ready' probe) would replace the polling dance once. Single strong instance this window — logging as a building block.
existing_mitigation: None found. No helper script or skill wraps the 'tail log until ready line / HTTP probe / timeout' pattern.

intent: shrink a working-copy repo that has ballooned to tens of GB because of build artifacts I don't need checked into the workspace
finding: The voquill working copy had grown to ~14 GB and the user asked Claude to figure out why and trim it to a 'release-target' build, 'removing all of the things that are dev only or for recompiling'. This differs from the prior disk-reclaim observation (which was OS-level cache purging) — it is per-repo build-artifact hygiene. If it recurs on another large JS/Rust monorepo it becomes a clear automation target (a `repo-slim` helper that surveys node_modules, target/, dist/, .cache, etc., reports sizes, and offers safe removal). One strong instance in this window.
existing_mitigation: None found. Prior disk-reclaim discussion covered system caches; per-repo build-artifact auditing is not covered by any skill or script.
